perturbation.original_text: WCC shall ensure that all WCC public communications shall make reference to XLI products as "Light Sheets", as further defined in Attachment A [Sponsor Benefits Schedule] attached hereto and incorporated herein by reference.
perturbation.changed_text: WCC shall endeavor to reference XLI products in some WCC public communications, potentially as "Light Sheets", as generally outlined in Attachment A [Sponsor Benefits Schedule] attached hereto and incorporated herein by reference.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear obligation for WCC to reference XLI products as 'Light Sheets' in all public communications. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'endeavor to reference,' 'in some WCC public communications,' 'potentially as,' and 'generally outlined'. This makes the obligation vague and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification could violate California's False Advertising Law if the 'endeavor to reference' is not fulfilled and leads to deceptive or misleading advertising. Section 17500 prohibits untrue or misleading advertising. The original text's clear obligation ensures that XLI gets the agreed-upon promotion, while the modified text allows WCC to potentially under-deliver, which could be seen as misleading to consumers who expect the 'Light Sheets' product to be advertised as agreed and thus be in violation of this law if it is found to be deceptive.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1. False Advertising in General [17500 - 17509] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) 17500. It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association, or any employee thereof with intent directly or indirectly to dispose of real or personal property or to perform services, professional or otherwise, or anything of any nature whatsoever or to induce the public to enter into any obligation relating thereto, to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated before the public in this state, or to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated from this state before the public in any state, in any newspaper or other publication, or any advertising device, or by public outcry or proclamation, or in any other manner or means whatever, including over the Internet, any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services, professional or otherwise, or concerning any circumstance or matter of fact connected with the propo
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity, weakening the obligation for WCC to reference XLI products as 'Light Sheets' in all public communications. This contradicts BPC 17500, which prohibits misleading advertising. The original text ensures XLI gets the agreed-upon promotion, while the modified text allows WCC to potentially under-deliver, which could be seen as misleading to consumers, according to the Law Explanation. The 'endeavor to reference' language in the modified text weakens the original obligation, creating a potential violation of BPC 17500.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the rights herein granted, and in keeping with XLI's desire to utilize WCC in the PROMOTION of its products and technology, XLI will pay WCC in accordance with Attachment B, which shall be deemed a part of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the rights herein granted, and in keeping with XLI's desire to utilize WCC in the PROMOTION of its products and technology, XLI intends to pay WCC based on Attachment B, which may be deemed a general guideline.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'XLI will pay' to 'XLI intends to pay' and 'shall be deemed a part of this Agreement' to 'may be deemed a general guideline,' the obligation to pay is weakened and made ambiguous. This allows XLI to potentially avoid full payment or dispute the payment terms based on the vague wording.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity that could contradict Nevada Revised Statutes (NRS) 104.2302, which addresses the definiteness of contracts. The original language clearly establishes a payment obligation based on Attachment B. The modified language, by using 'intends to pay' and 'may be deemed a general guideline,' makes the payment obligation uncertain and not a contract, potentially rendering the contract unenforceable under Nevada law because the terms of payment are not sufficiently definite and the agreement lacks contractual intent.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the payment obligation of XLI. The original text states "XLI will pay WCC in accordance with Attachment B, which shall be deemed a part of this Agreement". The changed text says "XLI intends to pay WCC based on Attachment B, which may be deemed a general guideline." The change from "will pay" to "intends to pay" and "shall be deemed a part of this agreement" to "may be deemed a general guideline" introduces ambiguity regarding the payment obligation, making it difficult to enforce. This contradicts NRS 104.2302, which implies contracts must be definite.

perturbation.original_text: Each party hereto agrees to execute, acknowledge and deliver such further instruments, and to do all such other acts, as may be reasonably necessary or appropriate in order to carry out the purposes and intents of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereto may consider executing, acknowledging, and delivering such further instruments, and undertaking such other acts, as they individually deem helpful to the overall spirit of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates actions 'reasonably necessary or appropriate' to fulfill the agreement's purpose. The modified version replaces 'agrees to execute' with 'may consider executing,' 'reasonably necessary or appropriate' with 'they individually deem helpful,' and 'purposes and intents' with 'overall spirit.' This transforms a clear obligation into a discretionary suggestion, weakening enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change could contradict California Civil Code § 1657, which addresses the time of performance of contracts. By making the execution of further instruments discretionary ('may consider executing'), the modified text introduces uncertainty about when and if these actions will be taken. Section 1657 implies that if no time is specified, performance must be within a reasonable time. However, the modified language makes performance optional, potentially violating the requirement for timely performance under California law. WCC is based in California, so this law would apply.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1657. If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed. If the act is in its nature capable of being done instantly—as, for example, if it consists in the payment of money only—it must be performed immediately upon the thing to be done being exactly ascertained. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code § 1657. The original text creates an obligation to perform actions that are 'reasonably necessary or appropriate.' The changed text replaces this with 'may consider executing,' which introduces uncertainty and optionality, potentially violating the requirement for timely performance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed.' By making the action discretionary, the modified contract undermines the legal expectation of timely performance, as the act may not be performed at all, thus contradicting the requirement of reasonable time as mandated by the law. The modification shifts a clear obligation into a discretionary consideration, creating ambiguity and making it difficult to enforce the original intent of the agreement.
